findings.message: This filter still admits public inherent methods as standalone `Function` nodes. The only gates here are `crate_id == 0`, `krate.paths.contains_key(id)`, and `Visibility::Public`, but method items are also registered in `Crate::paths` as `ItemKind::Function` with paths like `[crate, ..., ParentName, method_name]`, so they pass this check and get misclassified as top-level functions. That violates the documented 'standalone / top-level only' scope for `ItemEnum::Function` and will render methods in the wrong place.

`ItemEnum::Function` is emitted whenever the item is public and present in `krate.paths`, but that does not prove it is a free function. Rustdoc can also place associated methods in `paths`, so this loop will misclassify public impl/trait methods as top-level Function nodes and violate the standalone-function requirement. Exclude method IDs (or otherwise check the parent `Trait`/`Impl` item lists) before constructing `ExtractedNode::Function`.

`module_path_from_summary` collapses every middle path segment into a single `_`-joined string, so distinct Rust module paths can still map to the same node id. For example, `foo_bar::Thing` and `foo::bar::Thing` both become `foo_bar`, which means same-named types/traits in different modules can still collide and violate AC-11. This is a correctness bug, not just a rare edge case, because underscores are valid in Rust identifiers.

`module_path_from_summary` is still not injective: joining middle path segments with `__` only blocks the simple `foo_bar` vs `foo::bar` case. Valid paths such as `crate::foo_::bar::Thing` and `crate::foo::_bar::Thing` both collapse to `foo___bar`, so same-named types/traits in different modules can still share a node id and violate AC-11.

`type_rep_node_id` / `trait_rep_node_id` append `__self` without reserving that suffix. A real item named `Foo__self` produces the same id as the representative node for `Foo`, so an actual type/trait can collide with its edge-endpoint helper node.

`function_node_id` sanitizes the entire `full_path` as one string, which drops path-boundary information and can produce identical ids for different functions when module or item names contain underscores. For example, `crate::a::b__c::f` and `crate::a__b::c::f` both sanitize to `crate__a__b__c__f`, so the final `F<len>_...` ids collide even though the paths are distinct. This breaks the intended injective node-id scheme and can merge unrelated function nodes/edges.

`module_path_from_summary` does not return the `sanitized_module_path` format required by the ADR/spec. It length-encodes each middle segment (`6__review__7__manager`), but the contract says the module path should just be the middle segments joined with `_` after sanitization (`review_manager`). As written, every type/trait id built from this helper will diverge from the documented `T<len>_...` / `R<len>_...` format.

`function_node_id` is also using a per-segment length encoding (`encode_path_segments`) instead of the spec's `sanitized_full_path` string. The ADR defines `F<len>_<sanitized_layer>_<sanitized_crate>_<sanitized_full_path>`, so this implementation will generate ids that do not match the documented format for any function path.

`function_node_id` (and the shared `type_trait_body` path used by `type_node_id` / `trait_node_id`) joins sanitized segments with raw `_` separators. Because `LayerId` and `CrateName` both allow `_`, distinct inputs can still collapse to the same node ID before the length prefix. Example: `type_node_id("a_b", "c", "", "d") == type_node_id("a", "b_c", "", "d")`. That breaks the collision-free guarantee and can merge unrelated nodes/edges.

Grouping Struct/Enum/Trait/TypeAlias entries by the short `name` key overwrites same-named items from different modules, so one of the colliding entries and its edges disappear from the rendered graph.

This call renders every crate cluster with the full `baselines` slice, and `emit_all_entries_for_layer` also extracts nodes from the full layer, so a layer with multiple crates duplicates all entries under every `*_root` cluster instead of scoping the content to that crate.

`field_type_node_id` resolves same-crate `Type::ResolvedPath` values to `type_node_id` (the subgraph id) instead of the representative `type_rep_node_id`, so struct-field, enum-payload, and alias edges target the container rather than the concrete node the renderer creates for edge endpoints.

`render_clusters_mermaid` builds one output per crate (`cluster_key`), but `emit_all_entries_for_layer` is called with the full `baselines` slice and no crate filter. Every cluster file will therefore contain all layer entries from every crate, not just the crate named in the surrounding root subgraph.

`emit_all_entries_for_layer` deduplicates structs/enums/traits/type aliases by bare `name` in a `BTreeMap`. If two same-named items exist in different modules, the later insert overwrites the earlier one, so one node and its edges disappear silently even though the node-id scheme was designed to disambiguate module-qualified names.

`field_type_node_id` returns `type_node_id(...)` for same-crate `ResolvedPath` targets, but these edges are supposed to point at the representative `__self` node inside the target subgraph. Local struct/enum/alias edges will therefore terminate on the subgraph container ID instead of a concrete node.

`build_entry_label` reconstructs the display path by replacing every `_` with `::`, but `module_path_from_summary()` already collapses real path segments with `_`. Any module segment that legitimately contains an underscore will be rendered as a different path (for example `foo_bar` becomes `foo::bar`), so the emitted subgraph labels can be wrong.

`VariantKind::Struct` payload handling ignores `has_stripped_fields`. When rustdoc omits hidden/private variant fields, this code still emits edges for the remaining field IDs, which produces a partial and potentially misleading enum-variant shape instead of skipping or marking the incomplete payload.

`emit_struct_subgraph` looks up `[edge.field]` as soon as it sees a non-stripped plain struct, before knowing whether any field edge will actually be emitted. An empty public plain struct, or one whose listed fields are absent/non-`StructField`, will return `RenderFailed` if `[edge.field]` is omitted even though K requires no field edges for that case. The tuple branch has the same eager lookup at line 124 for an empty/all-`None` tuple struct. Defer the `edge_arrow_label(..., "field")` lookup until just before pushing the first field edge.

`emit_enum_subgraph` requires `[edge.variant_payload]` before it knows whether any payload edge exists. For an enum with only plain variants, no variants, or all payload fields skipped, rendering fails on a missing `variant_payload` style even though decision H says plain variants emit no edge. Move the lookup into the tuple/struct payload branches only when an edge is about to be pushed.

This match only emits impl edges for `Type::ResolvedPath` and blanket bodies for `Type::Generic`. Legal trait impls on primitives, tuples, arrays, references, and other concrete self types fall into `_ => continue`, so their `-.impl.->` edges are silently omitted and the rendered graph is incomplete.

`resolve_trait_key` returns `None` for every non-zero `summary.crate_id`, so impls of traits declared in another workspace crate are never resolved through the global trait index. In rustdoc, `crate_b::Type: crate_a::Trait` is represented in crate_b with a non-zero crate id plus an `external_crates` entry for `crate_a`; this code treats that as unresolvable and `emit_impl_edges` silently skips the required `-.impl.->` edge despite the trait being indexed from crate_a's baseline. Resolve non-zero crate ids via `krate.external_crates` and let the trait-index lookup decide whether the trait is in-scope/workspace.

Blanket-body nodes are appended to `subgraph_lines` from `emit_impl_edges`, but the only integration call happens after all trait subgraphs have already been emitted and closed. That means `blanket_impl: None` + `for_: Type::Generic` renders as a disconnected top-level node, not near/inside the target trait subgraph as required by the T008 blanket-body behavior. The blanket nodes need to be collected and inserted while emitting the corresponding trait subgraph, before its `end`, or otherwise explicitly attached to the trait.

`crate_filter` limits rendered entries to one crate, but impl-edge emission still resolves targets through the global `trait_index` and emits the edge even when the trait subgraph belongs to another crate that was not rendered in this cluster. In that case Mermaid will create a dangling/phantom target node instead of a real trait subgraph, violating the cluster-internal edge behavior for `render_clusters_mermaid`. Filter the resolved trait target to the rendered crate or defer cross-cluster impl edges to the overview aggregation.

`build_trait_index` indexes every own-crate trait regardless of visibility, while entry extraction only renders `Visibility::Public` top-level traits. A public type implementing a non-public trait can therefore produce an impl edge to a trait representative node that has no emitted subgraph, again creating a phantom Mermaid node. The trait index should only contain traits that are renderable under the same visibility rules as `extract_nodes`.

`resolve_type_rep_node_id` accepts any own-crate `ResolvedPath` without checking the resolved item visibility. `emit_impl_edges` then emits a `trait_impl` edge from that rep id, but entry subgraphs are only rendered for `Visibility::Public` items, so an impl for a private own-crate type can create a phantom/private type node in the graph. Mirror the public visibility check used by `extract_nodes` before returning a type rep id for edge emission.

Inherent method emission treats `Visibility::Default` as renderable. The existing CC-1 exception for `Default` applies to trait methods and enum variants, not inherent methods; a default-visibility inherent method is private when present in the input, so this can merge private methods into a public type subgraph. Restrict inherent methods to `Visibility::Public`.

`resolve_type_rep_node_id` returns a rendered type representative id for any public local `ResolvedPath`, but the renderer only emits entry subgraphs for Struct/Enum/TypeAlias. A public `union` with a trait impl therefore passes this check and `emit_impl_edges` emits a `-.impl.->` edge from a `T...__self` source node that is never defined, creating a phantom Mermaid node. Restrict this helper to the rendered type item kinds before returning the rep id.

`render_overview_mermaid` now calls the full depth-2 `emit_all_entries_for_layer` just to harvest edge lines. That makes the overview fail on any baseline that needs a depth-2-only style key (for example `[edge.field]`, `[edge.alias]`, or method/blanket node styles) even though those lines are discarded and never appear in the overview. The overview should only depend on styles for the cross-cluster output it actually renders.

The overview renderer hardcodes `:::cluster` on every cluster node even though it reads the configured cluster class from `style.node["Cluster"].class`. That means cluster styling is not fully config-driven: a user-defined `classDef cluster` will be applied unintentionally, and a non-`cluster` class name is only partially honored.

`ClusterKey::node_id()` is not injective: it concatenates two already-valid identifiers with `_`, so distinct clusters can collapse to the same Mermaid node id when the crate name or top-level module contains underscores. The `"root"` sentinel in `cluster_key_from_path()` makes the ambiguity worse by merging a real top-level `root` module with crate-root items. That can silently merge separate clusters and misroute cross-cluster edges.

The `emit_all_entries_for_layer()` result is discarded and the scratch edge buffer is still consumed afterward. If edge-style resolution fails partway through rendering, the overview can emit a partial, misleading cross-cluster edge set instead of propagating the error or suppressing the harvest entirely.

`ClusterKey::node_id()` is not injective: it just sanitizes and concatenates `crate_name` and `module_seg1`, so distinct raw cluster keys can alias after sanitization (for example `a-b` + `c` vs `a` + `b_c`). `cluster_key_from_path()` also uses the literal `root` sentinel, so a real top-level module named `root` collapses into the crate-root cluster. That can merge unrelated clusters and misclassify edges.

`render_overview_mermaid()` suppresses any error from `emit_all_entries_for_layer()` and clears `all_edge_lines`. If a required edge style is missing or another render failure occurs, the function still returns `Ok(...)` with cross-cluster edges silently omitted instead of failing closed, which hides configuration problems and contradicts the stated error contract.

The overview renderer still emits inline `:::<class>` annotations for cluster nodes (`{cluster_node_id}["{label}"]:::{cluster_class}`) even though the T009 brief requires class application to be emitted only as separate `class <id> <className>` lines. That violates the mandated output structure and reintroduces the Mermaid parsing risk this step was meant to eliminate.

The overview reducer discards the original edge syntax and label when it collapses cross-cluster relations: every surviving relation is re-emitted as `-->` after deduping by `(src_cluster, dst_cluster)`. That turns explicit non-directional/typed edges such as `---|alias_of|` into plain directed arrows and can collapse different relation kinds between the same cluster pair into one arbitrary edge, so the depth-1 diagram no longer faithfully reflects the underlying graph.

`lookup_cluster` uses a raw string prefix match (`starts_with("{key}_")`) to classify edge endpoints. That can misattribute non-entry node ids such as `anon_*`, `prim_*`, or `generic_*` when their text happens to share a cluster prefix, which then produces incorrect cluster-pair aggregation or drops real cross-cluster edges.

Cluster nodes are emitted with a hard-coded rectangular shape (`[{label}]`) here, so any configured `[node.Cluster].shape` is silently ignored. That breaks the documented node-style API and makes the overview renderer inconsistent with the other node kinds, which do honor `shape`.

`ClusterKey::node_id()` builds Mermaid ids by simply concatenating sanitized `crate_name` and `module_seg1`, so distinct valid clusters can collide after sanitization (for example `foo` + `bar_baz` and `foo_bar` + `baz` both become `foo_bar_baz`). That would merge unrelated clusters and misattach edges/classes in the overview; the `

`ClusterKey::node_id()` is not injective for legal Rust names. Sanitizing each segment and joining them with `__` still collides when one side has leading/trailing underscores, so two distinct `(crate_name, module_seg1)` pairs can render to the same Mermaid node id and be merged into one cluster.

`cluster_key_from_path()` uses the literal string `"root"` as a sentinel for crate-root items. That makes a real top-level module named `root` indistinguishable from crate-root entries, so different clusters collapse together and their cross-cluster edges are attributed to the wrong cluster.

`render_overview_mermaid` calls the full depth-2 entry renderer before it knows which edges survive the overview reduction. That makes depth-1 overview rendering fail on depth-2-only style requirements, e.g. a same-cluster struct field with a style config missing `[edge.field]` returns `RenderFailed` even though the overview would discard that intra-cluster edge and emit no field styling. The overview path should classify cross-cluster relations without requiring styles for edges it will not output.

`collect_entry_edge_pairs` does not preserve the full rendered dependency graph for the overview: it only harvests struct-field and type-alias targets, then explicitly skips enum variant payload edges and trait impl edges. Those edges are emitted by the depth-2 renderer, so any payload or impl dependency whose source and target are in different clusters disappears from the depth-1 cross-cluster aggregation, violating the T009 requirement to emit deduplicated cross-cluster connectivity.

Cross-crate field/alias dependencies are dropped before clustering. `resolve_type_node_id` only maps `ResolvedPath` targets when `summary.crate_id == 0`; external crate targets become `anon_*`, and `render_overview_mermaid` then skips them because they cannot be found in `node_cluster_map`. If another `BaselineDocument` in the layer contains that target crate, both cluster nodes are present but their cross-cluster edge is missing.

Trait impl edges to traits below nested modules are silently dropped. `build_trait_index` keys traits with a `::`-joined module path, but this overview lookup builds the `TraitKey` with `module_path_from_summary`, which joins middle path segments with `_`. For a trait at `crate::api::traits::MyTrait`, the index contains `api::traits` while the overview lookup asks for `api_traits`, so `trait_index.get(&key)` fails and the depth-1 cross-cluster edge is omitted. That violates the T009 requirement to aggregate cross-cluster trait-impl edges.

`collect_entry_edge_pairs` drops trait impl edges when the `for_` type is from another baseline in the same layer. The resolver requires `krate.index.get(&p.id)` to succeed, but rustdoc external items are normally present in `paths`/`external_crates`, not the current crate's `index`. A valid cross-cluster impl such as a local trait implemented for a type from another workspace crate will be omitted from the depth-1 overview, violating the U-r3 requirement to aggregate cross-cluster edge groups. Resolve the type identity from `paths`/`external_crates` and let `node_cluster_map` decide whether it is in scope instead of requiring a current-crate index item.

The overview cluster node IDs are length-prefixed (`6udomain_review`) instead of the U-r3/briefing shape (`domain_review`, `domain_root`). The depth-1 Mermaid structure explicitly uses `<crate_name>_<module_seg1>` examples for cluster nodes, and the task briefing calls out crate-root clusters as `<crate_name>_root`; with this implementation every emitted cluster node, edge endpoint, and class attach line uses a different identifier, so consumers or tests expecting the documented overview IDs will not match the rendered graph.

`execute` writes the overview immediately after rendering it, before rendering the layer's cluster files. If a later `render_cluster` call fails, the method returns `RendererFailed` after `index.md` has already been persisted, so the required load -> render -> write pipeline can leave partial baseline graph output behind.

Depth-2 cluster generation is hard-coded to one root cluster per crate: `render_cluster` is always called with an empty module and the writer key is only the crate name. The spec/port contract is per `(crate, top-level module)` cluster with `<crate_name>_<module_seg1>.md`, so crates with top-level modules will miss cluster files and `written_file_count` will be too low.

`execute` writes each layer inside the same loop that renders later layers. If rendering succeeds for an earlier layer and then `render_overview` or `render_cluster` fails for a later layer, the method returns `RendererFailed` after already persisting the earlier files. That violates the intended load -> render -> write pipeline and the local guarantee that renderer errors cannot leave partial output; render all filtered layers into memory first, then perform writes only after every render succeeds.

The root-cluster key is emitted as just the crate name, so a writer following the port contract (`<cluster_key>.md`) will create `domain.md` for crate-root items. The track spec/ADR requires crate-root detail files to use `<crate_name>_root.md`; this will put root cluster output at the wrong path once the writer adapter is connected.

`layer_filter` entries are copied verbatim into `resolved`, so duplicate `LayerId`s are rendered and written multiple times and `rendered_layer_count` is inflated. That makes the baseline-graph workflow behave differently from the contract-map workflow, where duplicates collapse via membership filtering. If repeated layers are meant to be harmless input, this needs deduping before the render/write loop.

The new ID-validation behavior is not actually covered here: `test_execute_contract_map_with_invalid_track_id_returns_error` uses a temp dir that is not a Git repo, so `execute_contract_map` returns from `SystemGitRepo::discover_from` before it ever reaches `TrackId::try_new`. As written, this test would still pass if the new CLI-boundary validation regressed, and there is no negative test for invalid `--layers` input either.

Move the `TrackId::try_new` validation ahead of the git-discovery/branch guard. As written, a malformed `track_id` is still masked by `cannot discover git repo` or branch-mismatch failures whenever `workspace_root` is not a valid repo, so the new CLI-boundary validation does not actually run first. The added invalid-track-id test also does not exercise the new path because it uses a temp dir that fails discovery earlier.

Section F says every `Struct / Enum / Trait / TypeAlias / Function` entry is subgraph化, but the surrounding text treats `FunctionEntry` as a standalone callable node that is not subgraphized. This needs one consistent rule for Function entries.

Decision O indexes traits by `(CrateName, TraitName)` after reducing `Impl.trait_` to the last `::` segment. That drops module-path information, so a crate with two public traits that share the same short name will collide and impl edges can be misresolved or silently skipped.

CC makes `Item.visibility == Visibility::Public` the sole node filter, but rustdoc uses `Visibility::Default` for associated items of public traits and variants of public enums. Applied as written, this contradicts H/H' because public enum variants and public trait methods would be filtered out before they can be rendered.

The enum payload model is wrong for rustdoc_types: `VariantKind::Tuple` contains `Vec<Option<Id>>`, not `Vec<Option<Type>>`, and struct variant fields are also IDs to `ItemEnum::StructField(Type)`. The plan therefore describes direct Type edges where the renderer actually needs an index lookup for each field ID, which would mis-specify variant payload rendering.

The trait-impl resolution algorithm treats the first segment of `Path.path` as the crate name, but rustdoc `Path.path` is the path as used and may be short or re-exported (for example just `Vec`/`Trait`). This makes cross-baseline trait impl lookup semantically unreliable and can silently skip or misroute workspace trait edges.

The render-output summary says classDef definitions are derived from both `[class.*]` and `[edge.*]`, but this ADR's schema defines `[edge.<EdgeKind>]` as only `arrow` plus optional `label`, while `[class.<ClassName>]` is the classDef source. Since this ADR inherits Contract Map L and the sibling ADR explicitly excludes `[edge.*]` from classDef generation, this contradicts the declared style schema and would mislead implementation of the renderer output.

Decision D claims the node_id scheme is injective, but Type and Trait ids include only layer, crate, and the short item name. Rustdoc paths are module-qualified and Rust permits public items with the same short name in different modules; those entries can collide in a depth-2 cluster file, and Decision O repeats the same short-name collision for trait-impl lookup via `(CrateName, TraitName)`. The renderer needs full module/path identity for Type/Trait nodes and trait indexes, not just the last segment.

`sanitized_module_path` is defined as the prefix of `ItemSummary.path` before the item name, but rustdoc paths are fully qualified and include the crate segment. That makes crate-root items look like they have module path `<crate>` instead of `[]`, which conflicts with the later root-cluster rule and with the trait lookup algorithm that explicitly strips the crate segment. The node-id and cluster scheme would duplicate the crate name and fail to bucket root items consistently.

The plan says to render public `ItemEnum::Function`s as standalone Function nodes, but rustdoc uses `ItemEnum::Function` for methods and associated functions too. Since trait methods and inherent impl methods are also embedded under their owning Trait/Type subgraphs, public inherent methods can be emitted twice unless the design defines a module/top-level membership filter for standalone functions.

Decision D claims the length-prefixed node_id scheme preserves injectivity, but the ADR later acknowledges that sanitizing non-alphanumeric characters can collapse distinct names to the same value. Length-prefixing only disambiguates segment boundaries; it does not prevent sanitized-name collisions, so the design currently promises a uniqueness property it explicitly does not guarantee. Tighten the claim or make the collision-avoidance suffix part of the decision rather than leaving it optional for implementation.

Decision U defines clusters as the top-level module segment (`module_path[0]`), but the filename and mermaid examples use multi-segment cluster names like `domain::review` and `domain::user`. Those are different cluster identities, which leaves the implementation unable to know whether to group by only the first module segment or by a crate/layer-prefixed path. The ADR should settle one cluster key and use the same key for grouping, labels, and filenames.

Decision K describes `ItemEnum::Struct` as carrying fields in a single `fields: Vec<Id>` field, but `rustdoc_types::Struct` stores them under `Struct.kind`: named structs use `Plain { fields, has_stripped_fields }` and tuple structs use `Tuple(Vec<Option<Id>>)` with hidden fields represented as `None`. As written, the traversal is not implementable for the declared tuple/plain struct coverage and would mishandle stripped tuple fields.

Decision U defines the depth-2 cluster key and filename using only the crate-relative top-level module segment, but Decision A explicitly claims the renderer supports `1 layer N crate` inputs. In a layer with two crates that both have `review` (or root) entries, both map to the same `review.md` / root detail file and the same overview cluster, unlike the sibling Contract Map U-6d-iii design that keys module groups by `(crate_name, module_path[0])`. The plan needs either crate-qualified cluster keys/files or an explicit cross-crate aggregation rule to make the `1 layer N crate` claim coherent.

Decision O already fixes cross-baseline trait lookup failures as `silent skip` (`edge を引かない`), but Open Question R later says cross-baseline lookup failure fallback behavior is still out of scope for a future phase. That leaves implementers with conflicting guidance on whether lookup failure handling is decided or still open.

This section says it inherits `旧 ADR §4.5`, but the immediately preceding `旧 ADR` reference is the Reality View ADR `2026-04-16-2200`, whose layer-agnostic material is not §4.5. The invariant being cited is later named as Contract Map ADR `2026-04-17-1528 §4.5`, so this cross-reference should be made explicit here to avoid pointing readers at the wrong ADR section.

`BaselineDocument` is described as a "wrapper newtype", but the same decision defines and depends on it as a three-field document struct (`layer`, `crate_name`, `krate`). In Rust, a newtype is a single-field tuple struct; this wording conflicts with the intended self-describing wrapper design and can mislead the follow-up type-design phase. Rename this to a wrapper/document struct, or make the type an actual newtype and carry layer/crate metadata elsewhere.

BB treats `blanket_impl: Some(_)` as only an expanded duplicate to skip, while later claiming the user-declared blanket body is `blanket_impl: None + for_: Type::Generic`. In rustdoc, `blanket_impl` is the marker for blanket impls themselves, so this plan would skip the blanket impls it explicitly says should be preserved/rendered, making the blanket-impl rendering semantics wrong.

The Reality View style schema inherits Contract Map filters such as `include_function_roles`, but this ADR’s input is pure `rustdoc_types::Crate` and it explicitly avoids catalogue-only role/pattern data. A fail-closed config containing function-role filters cannot be evaluated from rustdoc-only input without reintroducing catalogue semantics, contradicting the stated Reality View design boundary.

Decision BB says `blanket_impl: Some(_)` is the blanket impl body and should be skipped for now, but the rejected-alternatives section says skipping blanket impls is the corrected-away error and that the user-declared blanket body is `blanket_impl: None + for_: Type::Generic`; the same decision also refers to non-existent Open Question AA. The plan therefore leaves blanket impl handling undecidable.

Decision C says the Reality View style schema has the same structure as Contract Map L, including `[role.*]`, but Decision L later says `[role.*]` and `include_function_roles` are intentionally removed because rustdoc input has no catalogue role data. These are conflicting schema requirements for the same config file.

The Reality View config schema is internally inconsistent: line 175 says the section schema is `[node.*] + [pattern.*] + [class.*] + [edge.*] + [filter]` and explicitly omits `[role.*]`, but the later L decision reintroduces `[role.<RoleName>]` at lines 263-267. Implementers cannot tell whether role entries are part of the baseline-graph style file or not.

The proposed `render_baseline_graph_cluster(..., cluster_module: &ModulePath, ...)` API is not sufficient for the cluster identity defined later in U, which keys clusters by `(crate_name, top-level module)` and names files `<crate_name>_<module_seg1>.md`. In a layer with multiple crates that share the same top-level module, this signature cannot select the intended cluster unambiguously.

The U-r1 rejection cites old ADR `§Phase 2 Scope Update §S1` as the source for the depth=1 vs depth=2 evidence, but §S1 is the typestate/entry-point measurement section. The depth comparison and `Default=2` rationale are in `§S5.1`, so this cross-reference sends readers to the wrong design evidence for the cluster-depth decision.

Decision B includes standalone/top-level `ItemEnum::Function` as a rendered node, but the depth-2 output structure only allocates space for entry subgraphs and method/variant nodes. Since Function nodes are explicitly not subgraphs, the plan leaves them without a placement in cluster detail output, contradicting the selected node set and making the renderer behavior underspecified.

Open Question R says cross-baseline lookup failure fallback is out of scope, but Decision O already specifies that missing ids/crates/paths and lookup failures are handled by silent skip. The same failure mode is both decided and declared open, so implementers cannot tell whether lookup failure should be silently skipped or deferred to a later error/fallback design.

`RenderBaselineGraphInteractor::new` is specified with only `renderer: R`, but the plan requires the interactor to inject three secondary ports: baseline loader, `BaselineGraphRenderer`, and graph writer. With only the renderer in the type contract, the usecase cannot load rustdoc baselines or persist depth 1/2 output without bypassing the planned hexagonal dependency boundary, so T002/T011 are internally inconsistent.

The ADR already defines `BaselineDocument` as a three-field wrapper struct earlier in the document, but this consequence bullet still calls it a `wrapper newtype`. In Rust, "newtype" implies a single-field tuple struct, so this wording contradicts the chosen data shape and can mislead the follow-up type-design/implementation work.

`plan.md` and `impl-plan.json` still describe the renderer work as `render_cluster`/single-cluster even though the updated spec, domain, usecase, and contract-map artifacts now require `render_clusters` returning `Vec<ClusterRender>`. That leaves the implementation plan internally contradictory and points T004/T010 at the obsolete API instead of the new symmetric contract.

`BaselineGraphRenderer` now describes `render_clusters` as enumerating clusters with an `external item filter crate_id!=0`, but the infrastructure artifact says the adapter filters to local-crate items only (`crate_id == 0`) before deriving cluster keys. Those two descriptions disagree on which `krate.paths` entries drive depth-2 cluster generation, so the plan is internally inconsistent about the renderer's source set.

The `RenderBaselineGraphInteractor` description says it renders the overview and then forwards only `ClusterRender` entries to `writer.write_cluster`, but it never mentions `writer.write_overview`. That makes the depth-1 `index.md` output disappear from the described pipeline, which contradicts the rest of the plan where the writer port explicitly owns both overview and cluster files.

IN-15 now says renderer returns cluster keys in either `<crate_name>_<module_seg1>` or `<crate_name>_root` form, but the same requirement still constrains depth-2 filenames to only `<crate_name>_<module_seg1>.md`. The generated plan/AC repeat that narrower rule, so root-cluster output has no coherent filename contract even though `ClusterRender.cluster_key` and the writer flow use the key as the file stem. Please update the filename rule to explicitly include the root-cluster key form.

T010 still defines the depth-2 filename rule only as `<crate_name>_<module_seg1>.md`, but IN-15/AC-14 now require root clusters to use `<crate_name>_root.md` as well. Since `render_clusters` returns `cluster_key` for the writer to use as the file stem, the implementation plan leaves crate-root cluster output without the same filename contract as the spec.
